video_title: Warsh Must Choose The Dollar Or The Bond Market | Luke Gromen
gist: Luke Gromen argues that incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh faces a forced binary choice between defending the dollar or the bond market, and that an ill-timed inflationary Iran war (with Hormuz likely closed through fall) has wrecked the Treasury-coordinated 'disinflationary growth' plan. He sees capital-flight price action (stocks, bonds, and dollar all down), expects the physical world to crack the financial world within 1-2 months, and is near-term bearish on everything including gold and Bitcoin while remaining secularly bullish on hard assets as debt gets monetized.

themes.theme: Warsh's Forced Choice: Dollar vs Bond Market

insights.claim: Kevin Warsh's core constraint is a binary choice between defending the dollar (accept higher rates) or the bond market (suppress rates), because US debt is too high and there is not enough private balance sheet to finance it without the Fed; he will ultimately sacrifice one.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Frames the entire Fed regime as a forced trade-off; investors should watch the June meeting for which side Warsh signals he will protect.
insights.quote: It's a very simple choice. The dollar or the bond market. they're going to have to sacrifice one.

insights.claim: Warsh will likely sell the 'disinflationary growth' fairy tale (AI/tech-driven 1990s-style growth that doesn't lift rates) to ride two horses with one ass, but Gromen calls it total BS that only becomes true in 5-10 years, not now.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Positions the official disinflation narrative as cover; expect policy to be inflationary in practice regardless of messaging.
insights.quote: show me a data center that's cheaper today than it was a year ago, two years ago to build

insights.claim: The Fed will not stand aside and let the 10-year run from 4.6% toward 5-6-7%; faced with bond-market dysfunction Warsh will step in and buy bonds, so the Fed will effectively merge with the Treasury rather than be independent, which is inflationary.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Implies a 'Warsh put' on the bond market; the question is the yield level that triggers intervention, not whether it comes.
insights.quote: the odds of that are zero. That's never going to happen. So then what we're really talking about... is where's the worst put on the bond market

insights.claim: A divided committee (even former dove Waller turning hawkish) means near-term pain across all markets via a 'no atheists in foxholes' / TARP-vote dynamic, where a market crash is required before policymakers capitulate and ease.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Expect markets to have to break first to force the pivot; don't expect pre-emptive easing into hawkish committee unity.

insights.claim: The US deficit is driven by three untouchable items, interest (rising on the Iran war), entitlements (65 million boomers), and defense (budget going from $1T to $1.5T), so none get cut and rates must rise, creating bond-market dysfunction at 122% debt-to-GDP and 6% deficits.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Quantifies why fiscal consolidation is politically impossible, locking in either much higher rates or eventual monetization.
insights.quote: the deficit is primarily three things. its interest... entitlements... And there's defense

insights.claim: Warsh's pre-war plan was a coherent package: cut the front end (disinflation story), shrink the Fed balance sheet to steepen the curve, then deregulate banks (remove SLR-style leverage limits) so banks buy the Treasuries the Fed sells, which is just QE done through the banks.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Bank deregulation is a stealth Treasury-absorption mechanism; watch SLR/leverage rule changes as the real liquidity lever, not balance-sheet headlines.
insights.quote: It's just QE through the banks. That's it.

insights.claim: Scott Bessant's stated 'three arrows' plan and his 2024 criticism of front-end issuance collapsed once in office; he doubled the rate of Treasury buybacks, showing the fiscal reality is worse than outsiders thought.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Buyback acceleration is a tell that the Treasury is already managing dysfunction; track buyback pace as a stress gauge.
insights.quote: He didn't say that. But... He doubles the rate of treasury buybacks

insights.claim: The Iran war undercut the entire strategy because after 2.5 years of shifting issuance to the front end, an inflationary war pushes front-end rates up, which is self-defeating and balloons the deficit.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Front-loaded issuance means rate spikes hit the deficit fast (6% to 8-10%), accelerating the debt-spiral risk.
insights.quote: If you're going to spend three years, two and a half years shifting issuance to the front end... you can't be stupid and start an inflationary war that sends a front end up

insights.claim: Despite an energy supply shock that should boost the dollar, the dollar is flat-to-down alongside falling stocks and bonds, which is classic capital-flight price action (money leaving the dollar for commodities or debt paydown) rather than safe-haven behavior.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: A weak dollar in a risk-off energy shock is a warning sign; it mirrors post-Liberation-Day flight and signals stress, not strength.
insights.quote: market action of dollar down, bonds down, stocks down is capital flight. That's money leaving the dollar.

insights.claim: Foreigners hold ~$27 trillion net in dollar assets including $9.5 trillion in Treasuries against ~$13-14 trillion in dollar borrowings; if rates spike on oil, they will sell Treasuries to raise dollars for oil, triggering a debt spiral.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Identifies forced foreign Treasury selling as the transmission mechanism into a debt spiral; watch foreign holdings and the front end together.
insights.quote: What do you think they're going to sell to raise dollars?... They're gonna sell treasuries and you're gonna go into a debt spiral.

insights.claim: Gromen expects Iran to keep Hormuz closed through fall, with oil tank bottoms hit between now and Labor Day causing oil charts to spike, and the physical world to start 'kicking the financial world in the head' within 1-2 months.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: A specific timeline for an oil spike that would force a rate-hike/inflation reckoning; position for energy upside and broad risk-off into fall.
insights.quote: I think Hormuz stays closed through fall. I think sometime between now and Labor Day, people start to hit tank bottoms

insights.claim: China has cut oil imports by 4-5 million barrels/day without economic collapse, aided by stockpiles (~1.4-1.8 billion barrels SPR), surging EV/grid usage (Chinese charging up ~55% year-over-year), giving it far more flexibility than the consensus 'China is screwed' view.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: China can absorb a blockade and may prefer Hormuz stay closed to break Western bond markets; bet against the 'China loses most' consensus.
insights.quote: China's China's going to drop oil imports by four to five million barrels a day... not economically collapse

insights.claim: The failed Xi-Trump summit (no Trump bragging afterward), a downed F-15 reportedly hit by a Chinese shoulder-fired missile, and Chinese long-range radars detecting US stealth signal China is quietly aiding Iran and content to let the standoff run.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Reads geopolitical tea leaves as China playing a strategic long game; reduces odds of a near-term off-ramp, supporting the oil-spike thesis.
insights.quote: that F-15 didn't kill itself right

themes.theme: Financial Warfare: Yen, Won, Swap Lines & Petro-Gold

insights.claim: Japan and Korea have traded like emerging markets since late 2024: higher relative 10-year yields versus JGB/Korea drive weaker yen and won, signaling markets see them heading toward debt crises that will pull down the rest of the world.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: EM-style currency behavior in two major Asian economies is an early-warning indicator of a global debt crisis; watch the yield-spread vs FX-cross relationship.
insights.quote: Japan and and Korea are heading towards debt crises that are going to sort of it's not good for anything

insights.claim: US dollar swap lines are a far weaker weapon than a decade ago because China has yuan swap lines with ~185 countries; the UAE leveraged this to extract US swap lines (threatening yuan pricing) and then left OPEC to ramp production for Bessant.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: The US monopoly on financing is broken; counterparties now have a second bidder (China), shifting negotiating power and weakening dollar coercion.
insights.quote: China having swap lines with everybody except the Americans

insights.claim: If oil is priced in yuan and settled in gold (China has offshore yuan clearing banks at every major gold hub), producers no longer need OPEC to restrict supply; the incentive flips to maximize production and accumulate cheap gold before it re-rates from ~20 barrels/oz toward 40, 80, 200.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: A structural bull case for gold via a petro-gold system that would dissolve OPEC's cartel logic; UAE leaving OPEC may be an early data point.
insights.quote: what what we're really talking about is a petro gold system through the yuan

insights.claim: China continues buying gold as the price falls (weaponized three years ago to defend the yuan), the opposite of the consensus that China would be forced to sell gold, confirming a strategic accumulation rather than distress.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Persistent Chinese gold buying on dips is a structural bid that undercuts the bearish-China narrative and supports the petro-gold thesis.
insights.quote: China's buying just more and more gold as gold goes down

themes.theme: Near-Term Bearish, Secularly Bullish Hard Assets

insights.claim: Gromen is near-term cautious on everything because global bond yields are breaking out everywhere except China, which is bad for bonds, stocks, risk, gold and Bitcoin alike; gold and Bitcoin falling daily are signaling where equities are headed absent massive liquidity injection.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: Treats weakness in gold/Bitcoin as a leading liquidity-stress signal for stocks; favors patience and cash until liquidity is injected.
insights.quote: I think gold and Bitcoin are telling us something wicked this way comes uh for risk assets.

insights.claim: The adjusted Warren Buffett metric (total equity market cap minus US federal debt, divided by GDP) is now higher than 1Q2000 and 4Q2021, the highest in 65 years, marking the worst valuation setup of his career.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: A proprietary valuation gauge at a 65-year extreme argues for very poor forward equity returns; the prior two extremes preceded major drawdowns.
insights.quote: the adjusted Warren Buffet metric is now higher than it was in 1Q2000... It's the only time in 65 years.

insights.claim: Secularly, all arrows still point to debt monetization (the Fed will buy every bond it must to maintain Treasury functioning), which is ultimately very bullish for gold and Bitcoin once liquidity is injected, even though Gromen doesn't expect that injection yet.
insights.speaker: Luke Gromen (guest)
insights.so_what: The long-term hard-asset bull case is intact; the trade is to be patient through near-term pain and buy ahead of forced monetization.
insights.quote: Fed will buy every bond they have to to maintain Treasury functioning. Do not kid yourself. They'll monetize it all if they have to
